title: [Escalation] Excessive client reattempts causing 5xx/429 spike
preview:
From: Jake Harper <jake@fluxware.com>
To: support@redwood.com
Cc: ops@fluxware.com
Date: 2026-12-10T09:18:00Z
Subject: Production errors and timeouts — sudden 5xx/429 spike

Hi team,

We're seeing a sudden spike of 5xx and 429 responses from Redwood for a subset of our traffic starting around 09:00 UTC this morning. The error rate went from ~0.2% to ~8% over the course of ~20 minutes and our throughput dropped by ~40% due to client timeouts and retries. We're attaching a small sample of logs and a short pcap of retry bursts.

What we can observe on our side:
- Lots of repeated POSTs to /v1/generate within milliseconds of each other.
- Client retries appear to be immediate — no exponential backoff.
- Timeouts on some requests, then immediate reattempts.

Impact: multiple checkout flows failing; two customers reported payment errors. This is producing a lot of noise in our SRE alerts.

Can you confirm whether there was any configuration change on your side or if this matches a known incident pattern? We need an urgent mitigation plan — please advise next steps.

Thanks,
Jake Harper
Senior Platform Engineer | Fluxware
[Attachment: fluxware-logs-20261210.zip (application/zip)]

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Jake Harper <jake@fluxware.com>
Cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T09:45:00Z
Subject: Re: Production errors and timeouts — sudden 5xx/429 spike

Hi Jake — thanks for the quick report and the logs.

We are investigating immediately. Quick notes from triage so far:
- No config rollouts on our production fleet at 09:00 UTC on our side.
- We see a correlated increase in short-lived connections and bursty traffic from a small set of client IPs that match Fluxware's traffic patterns.
- Initial hypothesis: client-side retry logic without backoff causing amplified load and transient overload on a subset of endpoints, resulting in 5xx/429 cascades.

Requests:
1) Can you share the SDK version and client retry config (maxRetries, backoff, jitter) for the services hitting /v1/generate?
2) Please confirm whether you are on one of our SDKs or a custom wrapper.

We're CC'ing Stephanie from Customer Success and Ben (your AE) to align on escalation and next steps. We'll keep this thread as the canonical channel.

Camila Reyes
Technical Support — Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference | camila@redwood.com


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Jake Harper <jake@fluxware.com>, Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T10:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Production errors and timeouts — sudden 5xx/429 spike

Jake — I'm escalating this to our incident path. I'm assigning internal owners and getting SLA/credit options ready in case there's customer impact.

Immediate plan:
- Camila/Eng: confirm root cause and provide a short-term mitigation (rate-limiting or token bucket on our edge) within 2 hours.
- Stephanie/CS: notify Fluxware ops and coordinate status updates to their stakeholders.
- I'll open INF-4821 in Jira with priority P1 and track credits/contract implications.

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
[HubSpot: HS-Fluxware-231]

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Jake Harper <jake@fluxware.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T11:22:00Z
Subject: Re: Production errors and timeouts — sudden 5xx/429 spike

Hi Jake — thanks for the SDK details you sent (v2.1.0). After running the attached log sample through our analyzer we see the pattern you described: immediate reattempts inside client code with maxRetries set to 5 and backoff set to 0ms in your wrapper. That combination effectively turns transient 502/504s into a retry storm against the same shard.

Proposed immediate mitigations (to be executed in the next 30–60 minutes):
- On your side: patch the client to enable exponential backoff with jitter. Suggested defaults: initialDelay=200ms, factor=2.0, maxDelay=5s, jitter=±25%. We attach a small patch for your SDK (sdk-config.json.patch).
- On our side: deploy a short-lived, aggressive token-bucket rate limiter for Fluxware client tokens to smooth bursts and prevent cascade failures while you roll out the fix.
- We'll also add a temporary 429 -> 2s Retry-After header for overloaded endpoints to signal clients to back off.

Attachment: sdk-config.json.patch

I'll create a runbook for the handoff and cutover; Stephanie will coordinate the deployment window and customer comms.

On Tue, Dec 10, 2026 at 09:45 Jake Harper <jake@fluxware.com> wrote:
> Thanks — attaching the logs.
>
> Jake

Camila Reyes
Technical Support — Redwood Inference


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
To: Jake Harper <jake@fluxware.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-15T16:42:00Z
Subject: RE: Production errors and timeouts — sudden 5xx/429 spike

Jake/Fluxware team — following up with the closure note for INF-4821.

Summary of root cause:
- Fluxware's wrapper around the Redwood SDK had maxRetries=5 and backoff=0ms for a subset of service calls, which converted transient backend timeouts into a client-driven retry storm. The amplified traffic tripped our per-shard protection leading to increased 502/504 and 429 responses.

Actions completed:
- Fluxware applied the SDK patch to enable exponential backoff with jitter (v2.1.1). Rollout started 2026-12-12 02:00 UTC and completed 2026-12-13 09:00 UTC across their fleet.
- Redwood deployed a token-bucket rate limiter and short-term 429 Retry-After signaling on 2026-12-10 12:15 UTC to blunt the storm.
- We validated stabilized error rates: moving from 8% peak down to 0.25% sustained; latency returned to baseline within 6 hours of mitigations.

Permanent recommendations:
1) SDKs should ship with safe retry defaults (exponential backoff + jitter) and warnings in logs when backoff is disabled.
2) We'll add a server-side advisory header (X-Redwood-Retry-Recommendation) for client libraries that are sending excessive reattempts.
3) Provide a lightweight client-side health check sample that toggles retry aggressiveness based on observed 429 rates.

Open items: INF-4821 remains open for a policy update and documentation changes; target completion 2027-01-10. Ben/Stephanie — please coordinate the credits offer for the impacted SLA window and confirm in HubSpot.

Rishi Malhotra
VP, Customer Reliability | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: mitigation-runbook.md]

title: EU entity payment flow & VAT residency — next steps
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Emma Russo <emma.russo@merchantco.eu>, AP Finance <ap@clientco.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Dec 2026 09:07:00 +0000
Subject: EU entity payment flow & VAT residency — next steps

Hi Emma / AP team,

Thanks again for the productive call last week. We want to make sure Redwood invoices and payment routing are aligned with MerchantCo EU's VAT residency to avoid delays on settlement. A few items we need to confirm before we finalize the December invoice: 

- Can you confirm the VAT ID that should appear on the invoice and provide the official VAT certificate (PDF)?
- Is MerchantCo set up to receive self-billed invoices, or do you require Redwood to issue invoices that include VAT and a reverse-charge notation?
- If reverse-charge applies, would your AP team accept a single invoice with the reverse-charge treatment, or do you prefer we split platform fees vs pass-through charges into separate lines/invoices for cross-border reporting?

If you can share the VAT certificate and the AP contact for routing, we'll generate a provisional settlement matrix and sample invoice for your review.

Appreciate a quick confirmation — we have a payment window next week and want to prevent a hold.

Best,
Ava Chen
Account Lead, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0182

Attachments: none


From: Emma Russo <emma.russo@merchantco.eu>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: AP Finance <ap@clientco.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Dec 2026 11:24:00 +0000
Subject: Re: EU entity payment flow & VAT residency — next steps

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the note — attaching our VAT certificate. MerchantCo EU is registered in Germany: VAT DE274839102. Please use that ID on invoices. We are NOT set up for self-billing from Redwood at the moment; our AP requires vendor-issued invoices with a clear tax treatment line.

On reverse-charge: we typically accept the reverse-charge annotation on inbound invoices for B2B cross-border services, but our AP has asked for the following to avoid manual holds:

- Invoice must show: our VAT ID, vendor VAT ID, a statement: \"Reverse-charge - EU VAT to be accounted for by the customer per Article 194 EU VAT Directive\"
- A separate invoice line for reimbursable pass-through costs (if any) with net amounts only
- PDF copy of VAT certificate attached to the payment request (helps automated checks)

AP contact for routing and validation: finance.ap@merchantco.eu (they run the automated VAT checks).

I've attached MerchantCo_VAT_certificate.pdf.

If it helps, happy to join a 20-mins call tomorrow to walk through the fields.

Best,
Emma Russo
Head of Finance Operations
MerchantCo EU
emma.russo@merchantco.eu
+49 30 5556 7890

Attachment: MerchantCo_VAT_certificate.pdf (PDF, 120KB)


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Dec 2026 13:10:00 +0000
Subject: Fwd: Re: EU entity payment flow & VAT residency — next steps

Ava / Priyom — forwarding Emma's response (below). Quick internal note:

- This is a classic reverse-charge case for platform services: we should mark invoices as reverse-charge and include MerchantCo's VAT ID and the statement Emma mentioned.
- For automation and to reduce AP friction, propose we send two things to MerchantCo: (1) single vendor invoice with reverse-charge and a separate invoice line for pass-through amounts (net only); and (2) a short \"settlement matrix\" that maps charges to jurisdictions and tax treatment — see attached draft.

Operational asks: 
- Priyom: please review the settlement matrix and flag any charge types that require different treatment (e.g., marketplace fees vs third-party pass-through).
- Ava: can you confirm whether Marketing/Platform credits are treated as discounts or separate taxable lines on our end (that affects invoicing labels)?
- Legal/tax may want to review the wording for the reverse-charge statement — I can loop them in if needed.

If MerchantCo's AP still rejects after we include the certificate and wording, we should offer a Payslip (two-line invoice) as a fallback.

Thanks,
Marcus
Director, Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference
marcus_lin@redwood.com

title: Rivermark capacity pulse & SLA flex request
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Maxine Liu <maxine.liu@rivermark.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 09:14:00 -0800
Subject: Rivermark capacity pulse & SLA flex request

Maxine —

Quick note after our QBR deck: we saw the spike in token consumption you flagged (attached CSV). I pulled a short run-rate sketch and a couple of options that keep your latency SLOs while reducing unit cost for high-volume endpoints.

Two asks:
- Can your team confirm whether the surge in mid-November was steady-state or a one-off campaign?
- If you want to explore a short-term commit + burst buffer for the next 90 days, I attached an SLA flex proposal (v2) with pricing tiers and a simple headroom model.

I can scope a 1-week pilot (no code changes) that validates the batching/prefix cache savings against your live traffic. If that sounds useful, propose Tue/Wed next week for a 30-minute runbook sync.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Success, Redwood Inference
(attachment: Rivermark_Q4_token_report.csv - 28KB)
(attachment: SLA_flex_proposal_v2.pdf - 180KB)

From: Maxine Liu <maxine.liu@rivermark.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Evan Chu <evan.chu@rivermark.ai>, Rivermark Procurement <procurement@rivermark.ai>
Date: Fri, 11 Dec 2026 11:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Rivermark capacity pulse & SLA flex request

Ben —

Thanks for the fast follow. We ran a couple of campaigns in mid-November (holiday prep) but the baseline also ticked up ~18% in the following two weeks as more teams onboarded the new summarization endpoint.

We have two internal constraints:
1) Procurement needs clear breakouts for committed vs burst usage.
2) Some endpoints are latency-critical for production search.

Evan is looped in to help with procurement scope. Can you show a runbook that demonstrates the pilot will not impact the search endpoints' p99? Also, would the pilot require client-side SDK changes?

Maxine
Head of Product, Rivermark Analytics
(attachment: internal-campaign-schedule.xlsx - 12KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Maxine Liu <maxine.liu@rivermark.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Evan Chu <evan.chu@rivermark.ai>, Rivermark Procurement <procurement@rivermark.ai>
Date: Mon, 13 Dec 2026 08:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Rivermark capacity pulse & SLA flex request

Maxine / Evan —

Short answers up front: pilot is low-risk and requires zero SDK changes. We can validate the p99 with traffic shadowing + a small canary route that mirrors requests to a sandbox serving group. Outline below (attached PNG for expected prefix-cache delta):

Pilot outline (3 steps, 7 days):
1) Traffic profiling (48h): identify top 10 endpoints and sequence lengths; no production traffic diverted.
2) Shadow canary (72h): live traffic mirrored to a dedicated sandbox pool; measure p50/p95/p99 and token-per-response delta.
3) Controlled rollout (2 days): enable batching/cache heuristics on a single feature flag for non-search endpoints, compare error rates and latency.

Key safeguards:
- Search endpoints excluded from canary unless you ok a paired A/B test.
- Auto-fallback to current model variant on any p99 regression > 5%.
- Full audit logs so your security team has the event trail.

Karthik — can you confirm the SLA flex pricing rows in the PDF reflect the dedicated pool and burst buffer we discussed (particularly the two-tier commit option: 250M tokens/month baseline + 150M burst)? If so, Evan/procurement: I can get a firm quote and a 90-day addendum to tie the pilot to the commit.

Ben
(attachment: prefix-cache-benchmarks.png - 45KB)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Maxine Liu <maxine.liu@rivermark.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Dec 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Rivermark capacity pulse & SLA flex request

Ben / Maxine —

Confirmed: the SLA flex PDF includes the two-tier commit structure Ben mentioned and the dedicated pool pricing assumes N1 GPUs with autoscale for burst. Notes:
- Baseline commit (250M tokens/month) priced at $0.0016/token for generation + $0.00025/token for embedding-heavy calls (blended rate shown).
- Burst buffer priced as a discounted overage band with a soft cap; we cap spend on bursts with an opt-in threshold so you don't exceed procurement approvals unexpectedly.
- SLA: 99.5% availability with p99 latency objective; any sustained p99 degradation over the pilot will trigger an immediate rollback.

Subject: Re: Rivermark capacity pulse & SLA flex request

Maxine / Evan —

Short answers up front: pilot is low-risk and requires zero SDK changes. We can validate the p99 with traffic shadowing + a small canary route that mirrors requests to a sandbox serving group. Outline below (attached PNG for expected prefix-cache delta):

Pilot outline (3 steps, 7 days):
1) Traffic profiling (48h): identify top 10 endpoints and sequence lengths; no production traffic diverted.
2) Shadow canary (72h): live traffic mirrored to a dedicated sandbox pool; measure p50/p95/p99 and token-per-response delta.
3) Controlled rollout (2 days): enable batching/cache heuristics on a single feature flag for non-search endpoints, compare error rates and latency.

Key safeguards:
- Search endpoints excluded from canary unless you ok a paired A/B test.
- Auto-fallback to current model variant on any p99 regression > 5%.
- Full audit logs so your security team has the event trail.

title: Proposal: split-demo coverage for CTO travel window
preview:
From: Joanna Lee <joanna.lee@nimbusbio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tim Reynolds <tim.reynolds@nimbusbio.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Request: shift Monday demo — CTO travel conflict

Hi Ben,

Quick heads up — our CTO just had a flight reroute and will not arrive in SF until late Monday evening. He was going to join our scheduled demo on Monday at 2pm PT. Given the travel, we have two options that would work for us:

1) Move the full demo to Tue 11am–12pm PT (preferable for the exec to attend)
2) Keep Monday's slot but expect the CTO to only join the first ~20–25 minutes for the executive overview; Tim (product lead) will stay for the deeper technical Q&A.

Sorry for the late notice. Can you advise which approach you recommend? If we keep Monday, can you outline who from Redwood will lead the technical deep dive if the CTO drops after the exec portion?

Attaching our prioritized use-cases so you can calibrate the agenda.

Thanks,
Joanna
Joanna Lee
CTO, Nimbus Biotech
attachments: Priority-list.xlsx (XLSX)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Joanna Lee <joanna.lee@nimbusbio.com>
Cc: Tim Reynolds <tim.reynolds@nimbusbio.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 11 Dec 2026 08:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: shift Monday demo — CTO travel conflict

Hi Joanna — thanks for the heads up and the priorities sheet. Totally understand the travel disruption. Two pragmatic paths here; both are doable from our side. My recommendation depends on how important it is for the CTO to see the end-to-end deep dive versus just the strategic overview.

Option A — Move session to Tue 11:00–12:00 PT
- Full team available (exec + SE deep dive)
- Best for uninterrupted demo and follow-up commitments

Option B — Keep Monday 2:00–3:00 PT with staggered attendance
- CTO (Joanna) joins first 20–25 minutes for exec overview and contract/ROI discussion
- Marissa (SE) will handle the deep technical walkthrough and live Q&A for the remaining 35–40 minutes
- We'll record the technical segment and provide a short 2–3 minute exec summary clip to Joanna after the call

If you prefer Option B, we'll prepare:
- A 10-slide exec briefing (attached) for the CTO
- A clear handoff slide at minute 20 so Joanna can leave without missing action items
- A recorded technical segment + timestamped highlights for quick review

Proposed next steps:
- You confirm A or B by EOD Monday so we can finalize slides and calendar notes
- If B, we'll send a modified calendar invite that flags the exec window and SE deep-dive separately

I've attached the exec-briefing and an updated agenda for your review. Calendar links below.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

Hi Lucy —

Thanks again for the conversation last week. As discussed, wed like to start a short Mutual Action Plan (MAP) to align on the renewal window, the evaluation items for the additional inference capacity, and an expanded commercial proposal that covers a new model tier for your forecasting pipelines.

Objective for this kickoff:
- Confirm the decision-makers and procurement timeline
- Share usage baseline and target uplift for Q1
- Agree on evaluation criteria (latency, cost per 10k tokens, inference throughput)
- Set close date and next milestones (contract/PO, security review)

Proposed close timeline:
- MAP draft & roles: this week (Dec 14–17)
- Evaluation & short POC: Dec 20–Jan 14
- Contract & procurement sign-off: target Jan 21

Attachments: Im attaching a short MAP draft and the usage export from November so we have a baseline. Please confirm the right stakeholders to loop in from your side (legal/procurement/engineering).

Immediate asks:
1) Please confirm the primary approver for budget and the procurement contact.
2) If the dates above look tight, suggest a preferred close date.
3) Any compliance paperwork we should surface now (SOC2 evidence, IP addendum)?

Ill follow up with a calendar invite that captures milestones and owners. Thanks — looking forward to partnering on this expansion.

Best,

preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>

Subject: Commit signal review — flagged discount portfolios

Team,

Quick flag: pipeline sweep turned up a small set of deals where exceptions/discounts > 18% are still marked as committed in the forecast. I ran a quick heatmap (attached) and it shows concentration in two segments: Q4 pilots and a single enterprise account (BrightGrid).

Asks:
- Vanessa / Ben: can Deal Desk confirm which concessions already have L2 approvals and which are still pending?
- Marissa: finance wants a recommendation on whether we should continue to count these as committed for the December close or move to commit-at-risk.

I think we should consider a short-term hold on new discounts >20% until we align on approval cadence — that prevents last-minute forecast surprises. Proposed deadline for decisions: EOD Monday (12/14).

Aditya Rao

Thanks — helpful summary. A few points from finance:

1) For the December close we cannot accept additional revenue we don't expect to cash. If Deal Desk cannot provide documented approvals, those line items should not be included in committed revenue.

2) For BrightGrid (RDW-2026-BG-47) — if the customer expects the discounted pilot to be billable in December we need an agreed acceptance date and signed SOW/PO. Otherwise move to Commit-At-Risk.

3) Practical policy: temporary threshold: discounts >20% must have a Deal Desk ticket and Deal Desk approval + signed commercial doc to be counted as committed.

- Thu 12/17, 10:00–10:30 AM

title: Private Offer: prepaid voucher + reseller margin for StellarBio
preview:
From: Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week. Procurement here at StellarBio is evaluating a private offer on AWS and we wanted to explore a hybrid structure: a prepaid voucher (commit) that Stellar purchases up-front for a 12-month term, with the ability to route fulfillment and invoicing through our reseller, CloudReseller.

Key asks from procurement:
- Can Redwood support an upfront voucher that behaves like a committed credit (redeemable against usage)?
- How would reseller margin be handled (pass-through vs. list-price holdback)?
- Any constraints for AWS private offers around invoice routing and tax handling?

Tom Wei from procurement is looped for finance details. If possible, could you send an initial sketch of mechanics and a rough impact to list pricing + reseller margin?

Appreciate it — Lucia
Purchasing Manager, StellarBio

--
On Thu, Dec 10, 2026 at 08:45 AM PST Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote: (previous thread snippet)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>, Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 11:04:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Hi Lucia / Tom —

Thanks for the clear list. Short answers up-front: yes, we can support a prepaid voucher-style commitment, and we can route fulfillment via a reseller, but the routing and margin mechanics depend on the marketplace (AWS vs GCP vs Azure) and invoice requirements.

Attached is a short draft that sketches two options:
1) Voucher as prepaid committed credit (Stellar buys credit; credits applied to usage against the private-offer SKU). Reseller receives a pass-through fee on redemption (recommended for tax simplicity).
2) Voucher + list-price holdback (Redwood invoices Stellar at a discounted list, reseller is paid from a separate settlement flow). This is possible but adds reconciliation overhead and longer settlement windows.

I've included a quick margin calc (voucher_margin_breakdown.xlsx) to show net cash to reseller vs Redwood ARR recognition impact. High-level notes:
- AWS private offers: we can be the seller of record or route via CloudReseller as the seller depending on the PO setup. Seller-of-record simplifies invoicing but changes reseller margin flow.
- For a prepaid voucher, we recommend a 12-month expiry and monthly burn reporting (dashboard).

Jordan — can you confirm ops feasibility for seller-of-record vs partner-seller flows and expected lead time?
Karthik — can you flag recommended margin guidance (cap or % band) for Stellar-sized deals?

Next step: I'll pull legal to draft a short SOW once you confirm which option you prefer.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
attachments: Stellar_PrivateOffer_Draft.pdf, voucher_margin_breakdown.xlsx


From: Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>
Cc: CloudReseller Ops <partnerops@cloudreseller.com>
Date: Fri, 11 Dec 2026 08:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Ben — thanks for the options. Procurement preference is Option 1 (voucher as prepaid committed credit) because it keeps Stellar's spend predictable. A few clarifying questions from finance:
- For invoicing, Stellar needs a single invoice that includes the prepaid voucher charge. Is Redwood able to issue that invoice with CloudReseller noted as the fulfillment partner?
- CloudReseller needs a clear monthly settlement file showing redemptions and their margin percentage for accounting. Can you produce a CSV export from the console?
- Any concerns about expiry / unused credit treatment from a revenue recognition perspective?

CloudReseller Ops — please confirm whether a 6% reseller fee is acceptable to you and if you need gross-vs-net handling.

Tom Wei
Senior Procurement Analyst, StellarBio


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>, Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Dec 2026 09:33:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Tom / Lucia — looping in ops and legal. Quick answers from ops:
- Invoicing: We can issue a single Redwood invoice for the prepaid voucher. AWS private offer metadata can include the reseller assignment, but for seller-of-record scenarios Redwood will invoice Stellar directly and concurrently push a settlement file to CloudReseller.
- Settlement files: We will generate a monthly CSV with: date, redemption_amount_usd, sku, invoice_id, reseller_fee_pct, reseller_payment_due_date. We can include a transaction ID to match AWS billing if required.
- Unused credit: Standard practice is to include a 12-month expiry with a 90-day pro-rata refund window for bona fide business reasons; otherwise unused credit is recognized as breakage per ASC 606 — we'll share suggested language for the order form.

Karthik — pricing: for a $250k prepaid voucher we recommend a 6% reseller fee in a pass-through flow. If Stellar needs a larger margin, suggest moving to a list-price holdback but note that adds ~30–45d reconciliation and potential tax nuances. Can you confirm internal approval for 6% for deals sized 200–500k?

Irene — can you provide the short clause for the order form re: expiry + breakage treatment?

Action items:
- Ben: finalize order form option selection with Stellar.
- Jordan: send a sample settlement CSV template and ops SLA.
- Irene: provide 2–3 sentence legal language for expiry/breakage.

Jordan Blake
Partner Ops | Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>, Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>, CloudReseller Ops <partnerops@cloudreseller.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Dec 2026 16:48:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private Offer: prepaid voucher + reseller margin for StellarBio

Thanks everyone — appreciate the quick turnaround. Lucia / Tom, based on your preference for predictability, recommended next step is:
1) Confirm Option 1 (voucher prepaid credit) and accept the 12-month expiry with a 90-day pro-rata refund exception.
2) Confirm reseller fee at 6% (pass-through) so CloudReseller can receive monthly settlements without changing seller-of-record.
3) I'll create an order form + SOW draft with the legal clause from Irene and include Jordan's settlement CSV template.

If you confirm in the next 48 hours, we can have a draft order form for review by Thursday and aim for a Dec 31 invoice date to hit Q4 recognition for Stellar.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Phone: (415) 555-0193
ben.carter@redwood.com
attachments: voucher_margin_breakdown.xlsx

> Jordan's ops notes attached above.

title: Stepwise prove-out: acceptance gates, smoke tests, and launch readiness
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lina Harper <lina.harper@mediphy.com>, Mediphy Procurement <procurement@mediphy.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T09:15:00-08:00
Subject: Stepwise prove-out: acceptance gates, smoke tests, and launch readiness

Hi Lina / Procurement team,

Thanks again for the time last week. Attached is a condensed MAP and the smoke-test script set that map our evaluate->acceptance->close timeline. High level asks:

- Confirm the acceptance criteria below (and owners) so we can lock the MAP
- Confirm legal/procurement milestones (DocuSign window + PO timing)
- Confirm any additional compliance evidence you need (SOC2, encryption attestations)

Proposed acceptance gates (short):
1) Integration sanity: API keys + sample payloads respond with 200 and expected schema (owner: Mediphy dev lead)
2) Latency SLA: 95th percentile <= 350ms for 1k token prompt under your baseline (owner: Redwood infra)
3) Safety/concordance: model accuracy >= 92% on your testset (owner: Mediphy clinical SME)
4) Data handling verification: KV cache & retention settings aligned to policy (owner: Redwood privacy)

Acceptance tests are enumerated in the attached "smoke-test-scripts.md"; each test maps to an exit gate and required evidence (logs, screenshots, sample outputs).

Tentative timeline (can shift if procurement needs more time):
- 2026-12-14: Mediphy to confirm owners + any extra criteria
- 2026-12-18: Technical dry-run (integration + smoke tests)
- 2026-12-21: Go / No-Go checkpoint
- 2026-12-28: Cutover to pilot (if Go)

I've attached MAP_Stepwise_Proveout_v3.xlsx with owners and task-level checklist. Can you review and reply with any blockers by EOD 12/14? If the window looks tight, we'll propose a later cutover.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com

Attachments: MAP_Stepwise_Proveout_v3.xlsx (spreadsheet), smoke-test-scripts.md (markdown)

From: Lina Harper <lina.harper@mediphy.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Mediphy Procurement <procurement@mediphy.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T13:20:00-05:00
Subject: Re: Stepwise prove-out: acceptance gates, smoke tests, and launch readiness

Ben —

Got the materials, thanks. Quick initial notes:

- We like the structure. We do need SOC 2 Type II evidence and a short note on encryption-in-transit + at-rest for data in our pilot (can be redacted). Please add a single-line blurb or a pointer to your compliance pack.
- Latency: our clinical app is sensitive; can we add a secondary SLA for 99th percentile (<= 700ms) as an informative check during pilot?
- Owners: I'll nominate Dr. Evan Shaw (Clinical SME) for the accuracy checks and shall loop him on the next tech dry-run.

Procurement: please confirm DocuSign cadence — we aim to issue a PO within 5 business days after sign-off, subject to minor redlines.

I'll review the MAP in detail by Monday (12/14). One ask: include a short rollback plan in the MAP (just 3 bullets) in case performance regressions appear in pilot.

Thanks,
Lina
Lina Harper
VP of Product, Mediphy
lina.harper@mediphy.com


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Lina Harper <lina.harper@mediphy.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Mediphy Procurement <procurement@mediphy.com>
Date: 2026-12-11T10:00:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Stepwise prove-out: acceptance gates, smoke tests, and launch readiness

Thanks Lina — responding with technical clarifications and a short rollback scaffold.

Marissa here (Solutions Engineering). A few details to make acceptance tests executable:

Smoke-test highlights (see attached .md for scripts):
- T1: Auth & schema validation — run 10 synthetic calls with expected payloads, assert 100% schema match, 0 5xx.
- T2: Latency sweep — baseline 1k token prompt at 1 RPS for 5 min; collect p50/p95/p99 and CPU/GPU utilization. Thresholds: p95 <= 350ms, p99 <= 700ms.
- T3: Concordance batch — run 500 labeled clinical prompts; measure top-1 match and F1 vs. your ground truth (target >= 92%).
- T4: Data retention audit — trigger store/delete cycle and request logs showing retention TTL and deletion event.

Evidence we will provide per test: raw request/response logs (redacted identifiers), aggregated latency histograms, and the smoke test runbook outputs. For SOC2 references, see our compliance pack link at: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1a2b3c-example (redacted view available).

Rollback scaffold (3 bullets per Lina request):
- Immediate rollback: switch routing to smaller-capacity model variant + enable per-route throttling for 30 minutes.
- Intermediate: disable caching, drain pilot pool, and scale-up dedicated nodes for further investigation.
- Post-mortem: capture traces and run the concordance regression harness; propose remediations within 72 hours.

Proposed owners (updateable in the MAP spreadsheet):
- Tech dry-run owner: Aisha Rahman (Redwood SRE)
- Clinical QA owner: Dr. Evan Shaw (Mediphy)
- Procurement/legal: Mediphy Procurement

If that aligns, we'll update the MAP spreadsheet and smoke-test scripts and push a v3.1 by EOD. Ben — can you confirm the Dec 21 Go/No-Go call time window (we have 10-12 PST open)?

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com


From: Mediphy Procurement <procurement@mediphy.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Lina Harper <lina.harper@mediphy.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-13T11:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Stepwise prove-out: acceptance gates, smoke tests, and launch readiness

Ben / Team,

Procurement reviewed the MAP and test artifacts. We can proceed with the proposed runbook with the following confirmations required before DocuSign:

1) Add a one-line statement in the MAP confirming 30-day log retention for pilot artifacts (we will request archival for audits).
2) SOC 2 Type II summary or a link to the compliance pack (redacted is fine).
3) Confirm PO timing: we'll issue PO within 5 business days of the signed agreement; please confirm any billing milestones in the DocuSign template.

We accept the Dec 21 Go/No-Go proposal. Proposed call time: 11:00 AM PST on 2026-12-21. Please send a calendar invite.

Once the three items above are addressed in the MAP, Procurement authorizes moving to DocuSign.

Thanks,
Procurement Team
procurement@mediphy.com

title: International fit triage: model recommendations + sample prompts
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lucia Rossi <lucia@linguae.com>, Tarun Singh <tarun.singh@payapac.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 2026 15:06:00 -0800
Subject: International fit triage: model recommendations + sample prompts

Hi Lucia/Tarun — thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we don’t lose momentum.

Recap (what we heard)
- Key locales: Italian (it-IT), European Spanish (es-ES), Japanese (ja-JP).
- Primary UX: short transactional responses (n/2 lines) plus longer help articles for JA/ES.
- Main quality concerns: formality register, code-switching with English, and high factual consistency for billing/customer prompts.

Redwood proposed eval plan (short):
1) Run a 3-model sweep per locale on a 200-sample prompt set you provide (anonymized customer snippets).
2) Metrics: acceptability (human A/B), factual consistency heuristics, and latency/throughput on our inference stack.
3) Deliverables: per-model scorecard, prompt failure modes, recommended prompt templates and a small prompt-set CSV you can use in UAT.

Candidate models we recommend for the sweep (examples; we’ll pick exact flavors that match your latency needs):
- Mistral-7b-instruct (baseline)
- Redwood-7b-alpha (cost-forward; good latency)
- Llama-3-8b-q4f16 (higher fidelity for formality control)

Timing ask: if you can share ~200 anonymized examples per locale and mark any PII-sensitive items by EOD Thursday (2026-12-03), we can run the sweep and share an initial readout by next Tuesday (2026-12-08). If you prefer, we can run on synthetic/redacted data first.

Permissions / compliance note: we’ll process the samples in a private evaluation environment (no external storage), we can sign a short DPA addendum if needed.

Next steps (simple):
- Lucia: confirm sample delivery method (SFTP/Drive) and timeline
- Tarun: any procurement constraints for pilot data access (if samples are behind a vendor agreement)
- Redwood (Neha + Ben): we’ll prepare the prompt-set template and initial benchmark script.

Thanks — I’ll drop the prompt-set template in a follow-up email so you can see the format.

Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0172

Attachments: prompt-set-template.csv (see follow-up)


From: Lucia Rossi <lucia@linguae.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Tarun Singh <tarun.singh@payapac.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 10:18:00 -0800
Subject: Re: International fit triage: model recommendations + sample prompts

Thanks Ben — that outlines exactly what we need. A few quick clarifications: 
- We can provide the 200 examples per locale by Drive (I’ll share a link with reader-only access); ETA is Friday (2026-12-03) afternoon.
- On PII: we’ll redact account IDs but keep semantic context (billing, product names). Is that sufficient for your eval?
- Timeline: readout by 12/08 works for me.

On another note, Tarun and I discussed prioritizing ja-JP results because our APAC operations want to validate long-form guide generation as well — is that included in the sweep or would that be a separate test?

Appreciate the help — Lucia

On Tue, 01 Dec 2026 at 15:06, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Lucia/Tarun — thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we don’t lose momentum.
> ...


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Lucia Rossi <lucia@linguae.com>, Tarun Singh <tarun.singh@payapac.com>
Date: Fri, 04 Dec 2026 09:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: International fit triage: model recommendations + sample prompts

Adding a quick technical plan and concrete model mapping so we can be explicit with Linguae: 

Proposal (technical)
- Per-locale sweep: run 3 model variants across the same 200-sample prompt-set. Evaluate on: human acceptability (blind A/B), factual consistency score (automated heuristics + spot checks), token-generation latency (p50/p95), and a regression watchlist for hallucination in finance/billing prompts.

Model mapping (first pass)
- it-IT: Llama-3-8b-q4f16 (preferred for register control), Mistral-7b-instruct (baseline), Redwood-7b-alpha (cost/latency).
- es-ES: Mistral-7b-instruct (baseline), RedAlpha-7b (if available; stronger few-shot for formality), Llama-3-8b-q4f16.
- ja-JP: Mistral-7b-instruct (translated prompt baseline), Llama-3-8b-q4 (tune via prompt templates) — note: Japanese long-form has historically required model-specific prompt engineering and occasional post-processing (sentence segmentation).

Risks / watchlist
- Quality regressions: keep an eye on recent model patch versions for ja-JP that introduced brevity bias — we’ll pin model builds for the sweep to avoid drift.
- Tokenization edge-cases for code-switched inputs (EN/IT or EN/JA) — we’ll add a small targeted test subset (20 samples) for code-switching.

Deliverables
- Benchmarks PDF with per-model scores + failure-mode tagging (we’ll upload to the Drive link).
- Prompt-set CSV (attached as prompt-set-v1.csv in the thread) with columns: locale, prompt_id, prompt_text, expected_behavior, accept/reject criteria.
- Short ops note with recommended production model and the quantization profile we’d use to meet your latency targets.

Owners & ETA
- Linguae: share samples by 2026-12-03 (Lucia)
- Redwood Evals: run sweep and deliver initial scorecard by 2026-12-08 (Neha)
- Product/OPS: produce production recommendation and estimated cost delta by 2026-12-13 (Monica)

Links:
- Eval runbook: https://redwood.inference/app/evals/DEAL-2026-0457
- Shared Drive folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-eval-pack

I’ll attach the initial prompt-set CSV and the lightweight benchmark script in the Drive folder; Ben, FYI I’ll pin model builds in the infra to prevent automatic updates during the sweep.

Neha Kapoor
Principal Applied ML, Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lucia Rossi <lucia@linguae.com>, Tarun Singh <tarun.singh@payapac.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Dec 2026 09:14:00 -0800
Subject: Re: International fit triage: model recommendations + sample prompts

Appreciate the quick confirm, Lucia — that timeline works. Final checklist before we start: 

1) Lucia: share Drive link with the three .csv files (it-IT, es-ES, ja-JP) and mark any samples that cannot leave your environment. If anything cannot be moved we’ll run the sweep in your VPC/private eval tenant — just flag it.
2) Tarun: any contractual gating (MFA, vendor access) we should know about before we ask infra to provision?
3) Redwood (Neha/Monica): we’ll schedule the sweep for 12/06-12/07 and deliver the readout 12/08. Production recommendation & cost delta by 12/13.

I attached prompt-set-v1.csv to this message (small CSV with example rows). Expect the Drive folder to be populated with the benchmark script and a short README.

If all good, Lucia — share the link and I’ll confirm ingestion pipeline (SFTP/Drive).

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0172

Attachments: prompt-set-v1.csv, benchmarks-eu-apac.pdf

title: Hosted API capacity top-up and tier remap for renewal
preview:
From: Ari Sokol <ari@fluxcart.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dana Li <dana@fluxcart.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T14:23:00Z
Subject: Hosted API capacity top-up & tier remap for renewal

Hi Ben —

We’re coming up on our annual renewal and our traffic patterns have shifted substantially since we signed up. Over the last quarter our peak sequence lengths increased and we’ve exceeded the Tier 2 burst window twice. We’re evaluating a monthly commit uplift to avoid throttling during campaigns.

Can you share options for:
- a stepped monthly commit increase (3-month ramp)
- how rate tiers will change if we move from Tier B to Tier A pricing bands
- any soft-throttle/overage protections you can offer while we validate the new baseline

Attached is a spreadsheet with our recent token usage and a note from engineering on KV/cache patterns. Also looping in procurement (cc) for PO timing.

Thanks,
Ari
Ari Sokol
Head of Platform & Integrations
FluxCart

Attachment: FluxCart_usage_summary_Q4.xlsx (spreadsheet stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ari Sokol <ari@fluxcart.com>
Cc: Dana Li <dana@fluxcart.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T17:08:00Z
Subject: Re: Hosted API capacity top-up & tier remap for renewal

Hi Ari — thanks for the data, that makes the need clear. I ran this by our provisioning team and sketched three paths below you can choose from depending on operational tolerance:

1) Staged Commit Ramp (recommended)
- Month 1: +30% monthly commit (soft reserve)
- Month 2: +60%
- Month 3: full target (+100% of current baseline)
- Includes a 72-hour canary with autoscaling burst enabled

2) Immediate Commit Increase + Burst Pocket
- Move to target monthly commit immediately; includes one-time prorated invoice and a 10% burst pocket for sudden spikes for first 60 days

3) Conservative Band + Auto-Upgrade Trigger
- Keep current commit, increase soft-throttle threshold and enable automated upgrade if sustained usage > 80% of current commit for 14 days

Rate-tier implications: moving to the higher commit band lowers per-token rates by ~18% for generation tokens, embeddings see ~10% uplift in discount. I attached a table with sample per-token math for each band.

We can also add a soft-grace (no-overage) window of 7 days after the change to let you validate. If Procurement needs formal wording I can loop in Legal to add a short amendment to the SOW.

Suggested next step: quick sync Wed (12/11) to pick the path and confirm invoicing cadence. I’m attaching an updated options worksheet.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachment: FluxCart_commit_options_v2.xlsx (spreadsheet stub)

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T18:12:00Z
Subject: Internal: FluxCart renewal — recommended concessions and ops play

Ben / Marissa —

Quick notes after reviewing Ari’s usage and Ben’s options:
- Recommend option 1 (Staged Commit Ramp) + 7-day no-overage grace. Minimizes financial risk and gives ops time to observe latency/kv behavior.
- Approve up to 10% one-time credit for onboarding if FluxCart signs the annual commit within 10 business days (use as negotiation lever).
- If they push for immediate uplift (option 2), require a 30-day notice before discounted per-token rates to allow capacity planning.

Operational asks for Dana/engineering: ensure KV cache priming run during canary; add latency tracing on the upgraded routes. I can draft the amendment language for legal — suggest we limit rate exceptions to the first renewal cycle.

-Kim
Kimberly Park
Director, Customer Success Operations

Ari / Procurement team —

Great — we’ll proceed with Option 1, with the negotiated changes below:

- 14-day no-overage validation window (instead of 7 days)

title: Auriga commitment: burst-scenario simulation & p95 tradeoff analysis
preview:
From: Sanjay Patel <sanjay@auriga.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-11-30T09:15:00-08:00
Subject: Dedicated commit sizing — can you run a surge scenario?

Ben —

Thanks again for the demo last week. Product loved the latency during the chat flows, and our finance team is ready to talk a committed reserved pool, but they need a concrete surge-scenario run so procurement can justify the extra buffer.

Specifically, they asked for:
- Tokens/sec estimates for our expected peak (we do a mix of 4k & 32k context calls)
- p50/p95 latency under sustained 10-minute peaks (concurrent conversational sessions)
- Recommended dedicated slab sizing and a suggested burst strategy to keep cost predictability

We can share request mix and a sample week if that helps. Can you or your infra folks run a short model?

Thanks,
Sanjay
Sanjay Patel
Head of Platform
Auriga HealthTech

---
Attachment: request_profile_example.csv (text/csv)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Patel <sanjay@auriga.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-01T10:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated commit sizing — can you run a surge scenario?

Sanjay —

Yep — happy to. Quick ask so we model this to procurement expectations: can you confirm three things by tomorrow EOD?
1) Typical request mix (percentage of short vs long context calls). If you already have the sample week CSV, drop it.
2) Targeted SLOs you want us to guarantee (p50/p95 token latency targets).
3) What finance considers "acceptable" headroom percentage (e.g., 30% buffer, 50% buffer).

Plan: I'll run a deterministic tokens/sec simulation for your week profile, then Priyom will translate that into GPU-slab sizing and expected p95 under a 10-minute sustained surge. We'll share an annotated spreadsheet and a short runbook for burst handling (queueing vs dedicated burst slab).

I attached a template we use for request profiles — fill in the short/long split and concurrency assumptions.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference

---
Attachment: auriga_profile_template.csv (text/csv)

From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-02T14:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated commit sizing — can you run a surge scenario? (internal calc)

Ben —

I ran a first-pass model assuming the sample profile Sanjay dropped into Drive. High-level assumptions:
- Model: open-llama-70B-quant (Auriga wants a quantized variant we validated)
- Avg token generation per request: 180 for short flows, 2,200 for long flows
- Short/long split: 62% short, 38% long
- Typical concurrency baseline: 220 sessions; surge scenario: +3.5x concurrency sustained for 10 minutes (770 sessions)
- GPU: A100-equivalent slab per 8-way serving instance (continuous batching disabled at low latency targets)

Results (deterministic):
- Baseline aggregate tokens/sec: ~48.6k TPS
- Surge aggregate tokens/sec (sustained 10 min): ~170.1k TPS
- Projected p50 latency (tokens): ~18ms/token (baseline), ~22ms/token (surge)
- Projected p95 latency (tokens): ~120ms/token (baseline), ~340ms/token (surge)

Mapping to slabs:
- Baseline fits on 9 slabs (8-way), with comfortable p95 under target (~120ms).
- To maintain p95 <= 200ms during surge, need 26 slabs (this assumes no KV-cache warmup and no model-fusion micro-optimizations).
- If procurement accepts a p95 up to 350ms for 10-minute windows, we can cover surge with 18 slabs + a configurable burst policy (pre-warmed pool + autoscale buffer).

Notes & sensitivity:
- Reducing average long-flow token generation by 20% materially reduces slabs required (down ~15%).
- Enabling cross-request KV caching for long multi-turn sessions reduces tokens/sec by ~12% during surge (saves ~3 slabs).

I'll package the math into a spreadsheet with the per-scenario slab counts and cost delta vs different headroom percentages.

Priyom
Senior Inference Engineer
Redwood Inference

---
Attachment: latency_calc_v1.csv (text/csv)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Patel <sanjay@auriga.ai>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-08T11:07:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated commit sizing — can you run a surge scenario?

Sanjay —

Attached is a detailed simulation (tabbed by scenario) and a short recommendation. TL;DR: to match Auriga's stated p95 target of 200ms during 10-minute surges we recommend a committed slab pool of 26 slabs with an optional 8-slab pre-warmed burst buffer (can be billed as reserved burst or on-demand). With a 30% headroom policy procurement would see a monthly committed footprint of 34 slabs.

Key numbers in spreadsheet:
- Baseline: 9 committed slabs -> p95 ~120ms
- Surge (10 min): 26 committed slabs -> p95 ~198ms
- Surge with KV-cache + model quant-fu: 18 committed slabs -> p95 ~210ms (slightly above threshold)

Cost & billing ideas for procurement: reserve 26 slabs as the base commitment and include an 8-slab burst pool that is billed at a lower committed rate for the first 6 months. That buys Auriga predictable spend while giving us runway to validate cache impact.

Next steps:
1) Procurement: can you confirm acceptable headroom (%) and whether the burst pool can be contractually defined?
2) Product: we want to run a 48-hour pilot with 8 pre-warmed slabs to collect real p95 delta when KV-cache is enabled.

Attaching: auriga_surge_sim_v2.xlsx (simulation) and latency_model_notes.pdf (assumptions & sensitivity).

Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference

---
Attachments: auriga_surge_sim_v2.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet), latency_model_notes.pdf (application/pdf)

To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sanjay Patel <sanjay@auriga.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-12T16:48:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Auriga commitment: burst-scenario simulation & p95 tradeoff analysis

Ben/Team —

Thanks for the detailed workbook. Procurement likes the phased approach (26 slabs committed + 8-slab burst). Two quick confirmations we need before term sheet:
1) For the 8-slab burst pool, can you confirm the billing cadence (flat monthly surcharge vs metered at on-demand rates)?
2) Can Redwood include a one-time pilot credit for the 48-hour KV-cache pilot to validate slab counts?

- Avg token generation per request: 180 for short flows, 2,200 for long flows
- Short/long split: 62% short, 38% long
- Typical concurrency baseline: 220 sessions; surge scenario: +3.5x concurrency sustained for 10 minutes (770 sessions)
- GPU: A100-equivalent slab per 8-way serving instance (continuous batching disabled at low latency targets)

Results (deterministic):
- Baseline aggregate tokens/sec: ~48.6k TPS
- Surge aggregate tokens/sec (sustained 10 min): ~170.1k TPS
- Projected p50 latency (tokens): ~18ms/token (baseline), ~22ms/token (surge)
- Projected p95 latency (tokens): ~120ms/token (baseline), ~340ms/token (surge)

Mapping to slabs:
- Baseline fits on 9 slabs (8-way), with comfortable p95 under target (~120ms).
- To maintain p95 <= 200ms during surge, need 26 slabs (this assumes no KV-cache warmup and no model-fusion micro-optimizations).
- If procurement accepts a p95 up to 350ms for 10-minute windows, we can cover surge with 18 slabs + a configurable burst policy (pre-warmed pool + autoscale buffer).

Notes & sensitivity:
- Reducing average long-flow token generation by 20% materially reduces slabs required (down ~15%).
- Enabling cross-request KV caching for long multi-turn sessions reduces tokens/sec by ~12% during surge (saves ~3 slabs).

I'll package the math into a spreadsheet with the per-scenario slab counts and cost delta vs different headroom percentages.

Priyom
Senior Inference Engineer
Redwood Inference

---
Attachment: latency_calc_v1.csv (text/csv)

Subject: Re: Dedicated commit sizing — can you run a surge scenario?

Sanjay —

Attached is a detailed simulation (tabbed by scenario) and a short recommendation. TL;DR: to match Auriga's stated p95 target of 200ms during 10-minute surges we recommend a committed slab pool of 26 slabs with an optional 8-slab pre-warmed burst buffer (can be billed as reserved burst or on-demand). With a 30% headroom policy procurement would see a monthly committed footprint of 34 slabs.

Key numbers in spreadsheet:
- Baseline: 9 committed slabs -> p95 ~120ms
- Surge (10 min): 26 committed slabs -> p95 ~198ms
- Surge with KV-cache + model quant-fu: 18 committed slabs -> p95 ~210ms (slightly above threshold)

Cost & billing ideas for procurement: reserve 26 slabs as the base commitment and include an 8-slab burst pool that is billed at a lower committed rate for the first 6 months. That buys Auriga predictable spend while giving us runway to validate cache impact.

Ben Carter

title: Payload route visualization and deletion responsibility - MedSys Q&A
preview:
From: Claire Reynolds <claire@medsys.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Payload route visualization and deletion responsibility - MedSys Q&A

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking our call yesterday. Procurement here at MedSys is collecting two discrete items we need before we can move forward with the security review: a high-level map that shows where customer payloads (requests + short-term KV/cache) may be executed (regions/hops), and a clear description of the deletion/erasure flow (what happens when we ask to delete data, timelines, backups, and audit evidence).

Specific asks:
- A simple diagram or list of regions where inference/embeddings can be executed for the Hosted API vs Dedicated vs Private (and the default behavior if no region is specified).
- Which subprocessor(s) touch data in each region (cloud providers, CDNs, monitoring vendors).
- Whether transient transit through other geographies can occur (e.g., routing through EU when origin is US).
- Exact deletion timeline (hot cache, KV, backups, logs) and where audit evidence appears in your console or a link to where we can retrieve it.
- Any export-control or cross-border notes we should put in our procurement packet.

If you have a short PDF + CSV inventory we can attach to the vendor packet, that would be ideal. Can you also indicate whether we need to upgrade to Dedicated/Private to guarantee no cross-border hops?

Best,
Claire
Claire Reynolds
Head of Procurement, MedSys Health
claire@medsys.io


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Claire Reynolds <claire@medsys.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-10T10:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payload route visualization and deletion responsibility - MedSys Q&A

Hi Claire —

Thanks for the clear list. I attached a concise map and a per-subprocessor inventory CSV to this message (redwood_processing_map_v1.pdf, subprocessor_inventory.csv). Highlights below; Jonas and Marissa are looped in for technical and contractual detail.

High-level routing (defaults):
- Hosted API (default): primary execution in US regions (us-east1 / us-west2) for US accounts. EU-origin requests are routed to europe-west3 when a customer selects EU residency; otherwise traffic may be served by the nearest available region for latency.
- Dedicated: customers can pin pools to a region (e.g., eu-west3) to avoid cross-region execution.
- Private (VPC / on-prem): execution stays in customer-controlled network.

Subprocessors (summary):
- Cloud compute: AWS (US regions), GCP (EU regions), Azure (select APAC capacity).
- Networking/CDN: Cloudflare (routing, DDoS protection), Fastly in select regions.
- Observability/metrics: Datadog for host/infra metrics (data aggregated, no customer payload stored), Sentry for error traces (redacted).

Cross-border transit:
- For Hosted API, network-level transit may traverse intermediate backbone hops; however, we do not intentionally route customer payloads through a third-party country unless required for latency/failover.
- If EU residency is selected, we route execution to EU-hosted compute and only use EU-region-compatible subprocessors by default.

Deletion / erasure flow (short):
- Hot cache/kv: purged on request; erased within seconds/minutes from serving nodes.
- Persistent logs/backups: retention default 30 days; backups may persist up to 30 days unless an enterprise retention is contracted.
- Audit evidence: deletion events are logged and surfaced in the Redwood Console under "Data Actions"; we can also provide export of the audit trail for procurement.

Next steps: Jonas will add technical details about cache/shard behavior and final confirmation on export controls. Marissa can share DPA / SCC stance and SOC2 references.

Thanks — happy to set a 30-minute walkthrough with procurement + security.

— Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

Attachments: redwood_processing_map_v1.pdf (diagram), subprocessor_inventory.csv (detailed roster)


From: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
To: Claire Reynolds <claire@medsys.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-11T11:20:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payload route visualization and deletion responsibility - MedSys Q&A

On Thu, Dec 10, 2026 at 10:45 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> High-level routing (defaults):
> - Hosted API (default): primary execution in US regions (us-east1 / us-west2) for US accounts...

Claire — quick technical addendum to make the procurement packet concrete. Below are explicit mechanics we can include verbatim.

1) Execution loci and transient hops
- Requests: TLS terminated at the load balancer in the selected region. If a customer does not set residency, the platform will pick the region for latency optimization (usually the region closest to the client IP).
- KV cache: per-region in-memory caches; when requests hit different regions due to routing, a cache miss will fetch from the model runtime and populate the local cache — this is transient; the cached prompt shards are ephemeral and not written to persistent storage.
- CDN/edge: Cloudflare may see TLS SNI and IP-level metadata for routing, but customer payloads remain encrypted end-to-end until the execution node (we control the termination).

2) Subprocessor mapping by region (high level)
- US: AWS (EC2/GKE), Cloudflare (network), Datadog
- EU: GCP (compute in europe-west3), Cloudflare, Datadog
- APAC: Azure (select regions), Cloudflare, Datadog
Note: the CSV includes versioned commitments per subprocessors; we maintain contractual flow-downs and Data Processing Agreements for each.

3) Deletion lifecycle and evidence
- Immediate actions: upon a deletion request for customer data (or when retention TTL expires) we remove entries from hot caches and the model-serving working set immediately; deletion events are written to the audit log with a unique event id.
- Background actions: material in persistent logging and backups is queued for erase; we surface an estimated completion timestamp. Default behavior: backups erased within 30 days; logs retained 30 days unless configured otherwise.
- Enterprise option: we offer short retention profiles (e.g., 7-day) and contractual guarantees with SLA'd erase windows for Dedicated/Private customers.
- Evidence: audit exports are available as CSV/JSON via the Console or as a signed artifact if needed for compliance.

4) Export controls & cross-border note
- We support SCCs in our DPA and can add EU-residency clauses. Export-control risks are minimal for inference payloads (no controlled technical data by default), but customers with regulated data should use Dedicated or Private to get contractual and network isolation guarantees.

If useful, we can provide a short one-page "procurement summary" that pulls the bullets above into a format suitable for your packet. Proposed 30-min slot: Mon Dec 14, 10:00-10:30 PST.

— Jonas
Jonas Weber
Principal Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
jonas@redwood.com


From: Claire Reynolds <claire@medsys.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-12T16:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payload route visualization and deletion responsibility - MedSys Q&A

Ben / Jonas —

This is great, thank you both. A few quick asks before we accept the materials into our procurement packet:
- Can you convert the technical addendum into a one-page "Procurement Summary" (we use this for our legal team).
- Confirm whether Redwood's DPA includes EU Standard Contractual Clauses (SCC) and whether we can get a signed DPA during PO.
- A link to current SOC 2 Type II evidence or an attestation we can provide to our InfoSec team.
- Confirm the default backup deletion window is 30 days and whether the 7-day short-retention option is available on Dedicated.

If those are good, please send the one-pager and SOC2 link; we can then schedule the 30-minute walkthrough — Mon Dec 14, 10:00 PST works for our team.

Thanks,
Claire
Claire Reynolds
Head of Procurement, MedSys Health

title: Reignite: next steps on inference architecture after Ignite Summit
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Park <elena@starkretail.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-05T15:23:00-08:00
Subject: Great to meet at Ignite — quick recap and suggested next steps

Hi Elena,

Really enjoyed our conversation at Ignite this morning — the demo at the retail innovation track was a great backdrop. Per our chat, Stark Retail is focused on low-latency in-store personalization for recommendations (sub-200ms SLO in region) and wants predictable unit costs across peak holiday traffic. A quick recap of what we discussed and how Redwood could help: 

- Goal: real-time personalization at point-of-sale and kiosk (<= 200ms median).
- Constraints: on-prem for PII, multi-region footprint, existing vector store (Faiss) for history.
- Suggested approach: Redwood Private in a VPC with a small Dedicated pool for store-level inference + prefix/KV caching and cost-aware routing to cheaper quantized variants during non-peak.

Action items I proposed:
1) Share a short PoC outline (what success looks like, metrics, timeline) — attached.
2) Quick technical sync with your infra/ML engineer to validate integration points (30–45m). Marcus (cc'd) can lead the architecture session.
3) Pricing/commit framing for a 3-month pilot to validate latency and unit costs.

I attached a short Ignite-deck and the PoC outline. If you’re ok with that sequencing, can we lock a time for a technical sync next week? My Calendly is below; Marcus and I are flexible mornings PST.

Calendly: https://calendly.com/aditya-redwood/30min

Thanks again for the conversation — excited to explore this with your team.

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0182

Attachments: ignite-inference-deck.pdf (12MB stub), stark-poc-outline.docx (3MB stub)

Date: 2026-12-06T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Great to meet at Ignite — quick recap and suggested next steps

Thanks, Aditya — great meeting you too. I shared this with Daniel (infra) and he had a couple of quick clarifying questions before we set the technical sync:

1) For the on-prem/private option, what does the control plane look like? Who retains keys and logs?
2) Can Redwood integrate with our existing Faiss index, or would you recommend migrating?
3) Any reference for handling cold-starts for new stores during ramp?

We’re roughly targeting a Feb pilot start to validate through the Q1 peak. Daniel is available next Tue/Wed morning PST for a 30m touch.

—Elena
Sr. Director, Data & AI | Stark Retail
elena@starkretail.com

On Fri, Dec 5, 2026 at 3:23 PM Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai> wrote:
> (original message)


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Park <elena@starkretail.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>, Daniel Kim <daniel.kim@starkretail.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-06T11:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Great to meet at Ignite — quick recap and suggested next steps

Elena — thanks for the quick reply and great questions. Answers inline and next steps below.

1) Control plane / keys / logs — Redwood Private gives you an isolated control plane. You keep customer-managed KMS (HSM/KMS options supported) for model keys and tokenization keys; logs can be shipped to your SIEM/CloudWatch/Splunk per our audit connectors. We can provide an architecture diagram in the tech sync.

2) Faiss integration — Redwood can call out to your existing Faiss index; we typically recommend keeping the vector store in your env and using secure connectors. Migration is optional and only recommended if you want to centralize KV caches into Redwood for lower latency at scale. Marcus can sketch the connector pattern in the sync.

3) Cold-starts — pragmatic approaches we use: pre-warming with representative prompts, short-term local KV warming for new store profiles, and conservative routing to a larger dedicated variant until caches are populated. We include these as config knobs in the PoC.

Next steps I propose:
- Lock technical sync: Marcus + Daniel + any infra folks (30–45m). Proposed windows: Tue 2026-12-14 9:00–12:00 PST.
- We’ll send an architecture sketch and a sample SOW for a 3-month pilot (deliverables + success metrics).

I attached the Ignite deck and PoC outline again; Marcus and I will prepare a two-slide architecture sketch to circulate before the call. If Tue 12/14 works, I’ll send a calendar invite.

Cheers,
Aditya

---
Aditya Rao | Account Executive, Redwood Inference

title: SLO readiness check-in: hosted comparison and next steps
preview:
From: Sam Green <sam.green@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priya Singh <priya@acme.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 15:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Quick follow-up on today's perf demo

Thanks again for the demo earlier — appreciated the clear breakdown. A couple of clarifying points from our side to make sure procurement and infra have alignment:

- Our current SLA objective for the assistant route is 95th <= 300ms and 99th <= 950ms end-to-end for short-turn prompts (~40 tokens). For longer context (avg 200 tokens) the p99 can be relaxed to ~2.2s.
- Peak sustained traffic estimate for Jan pilot: 450 QPS (mix of sync generation + streaming), typical concurrency per model instance ~60 active connections. For streaming-first-token we need <150ms for first token at p95.
- We use a 3 minute conversation window for context caching; please assume 6–10 KB of KV cache per active session on average.

Can you confirm whether the Hosted API test rig will exercise both cold-starts and warm-cache scenarios, and whether we'll get token-level timing for first-token and tail latency? Also, please share the incremental test plan you mentioned so we can loop in infra and procurement.

Sam
Senior Engineering Manager
ACME Corp

--
Attachment: requirements-summary.pdf (stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sam Green <sam.green@acme.com>, Priya Singh <priya@acme.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 18:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Quick follow-up on today's perf demo

Sam — thanks for the clear targets and traffic profile. Quick confirmations below; full test plan attached.

- Hosted test rig: yes — we'll run a 3-stage validation: (1) microbench (single-instance, synthetic prompts) to map kernel perf; (2) realistic replay (your production traces + synthetic spikes) to validate p95/p99 under concurrency; (3) long-run soak to validate burst behavior and cache efficacy. We'll include both cold-start and warmed-KV scenarios.
- Observability: we will provide token-level timing (first-token latency, per-token throughput), per-route p95/p99, and ingestion-to-first-byte (end-to-end). We'll also push per-request metadata to our tracing dashboard and provide CSV exports for your team.
- Targets mapping: our hosted baseline (modern quantized model on A100-equivalent pool) typically hits p95 ~180–260ms and p99 ~600–850ms for short prompts at moderate concurrency; streaming first-token at p95 is usually ~90–140ms. We'll validate these against your traces.

Attached: slo-test-plan.xlsx (test cases, metrics, data requirements), hosted-vs-incumbent-latency.csv (sample baseline). Next steps below.

Next steps (suggested):
1) ACME to provide 1 week of representative production traces + 1000 sample conversations (anonymized) — due Tue 12/15.
2) Set up a dedicated hosted test window (2 days) with a scheduled 3-hour ramp and a 1-day soak — target week of 12/22.
3) Redwood to deliver initial report within 48 hours after test completion; include token-level CSV and dashboard access.
4) Technical sync (30m) after initial results to triage any regression.

I'll get the test env details and a short-playbook to you by EOD tomorrow. Will that timeline work for your infra team?

Cheers,
Ben
Principal Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference

Thanks again for the demo earlier — appreciated the clear breakdown. A couple of clarifying points from our side to make sure procurement and infra have alignment:

- Peak sustained traffic estimate for Jan pilot: 450 QPS (mix of sync generation + streaming), typical concurrency per model instance ~60 active connections. For streaming-first-token we need <150ms for first token at p95.
- We use a 3 minute conversation window for context caching; please assume 6–10 KB of KV cache per active session on average.

- Observability: we will provide token-level timing (first-token latency, per-token throughput), per-route p95/p99, and ingestion-to-first-byte (end-to-end). We'll also push per-request metadata to our tracing dashboard and provide CSV exports for your team.
- Targets mapping: our hosted baseline (modern quantized model on A100-equivalent pool) typically hits p95 ~180–260ms and p99 ~600–850ms for short prompts at moderate concurrency; streaming first-token at p95 is usually ~90–140ms. We'll validate these against your traces.

Attached: slo-test-plan.xlsx (test cases, metrics, data requirements), hosted-vs-incumbent-latency.csv (sample baseline). Next steps below.

title: Recap and evidence: iCare security evaluation + hosting options
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.inference>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@icare.health>, Aaron Patel <aaron.patel@icare.health>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.inference>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.inference>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 2026 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Recap: Security evaluation & hosting options for iCare

Thanks again for taking the time today — quick recap and proposed next steps so we have alignment.

Key takeaways from our conversation:
- HIPAA coverage required; iCare needs an executed BAA before any PHI is processed.
- SOC 2 Type II attestation required for vendor selection; you asked for the most recent attestation and an index of audit evidence.
- Data residency: primary in us-east-1; egress restrictions in place for certain data classes.
- Retention / eDiscovery: 7 year retention requirement and ability to pull audit logs for compliance audits.
- Integration: EHR inbound/outbound channels (HL7 / FHIR) — low-volume but sensitive.

Deployment options we discussed (high-level):
1) Hosted API (fastest path): Redwood-hosted endpoints with executed BAA + optional VPC peering and dedicated tenancy for isolation.
2) Dedicated capacity: Reserved GPU pools for iCare with data isolation and SLA-backed throughput; supports customer-managed KMS.
3) Private (VPC or on-prem): Private control plane, can be deployed into your VPC or on-prem for strict air-gapped requirements (longer lead time).

Immediate asks from your side: timeline for procurement, whether iCare requires customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM), and the audit scope (internal vs third-party).

Next steps (proposed):
- I will share our SOC 2 attestation and a draft BAA for initial review (attached).
- Vanessa and Marcus will prepare a short technical appendix answering KMS, log retention, and PHI-handling specifics.
- If helpful, propose a technical deep-dive with your infra/security team next week.

Attachments: Redwood_SOC2_TypeII_Attestation_2025.pdf, Redwood_BAA_Draft_v2.docx, network_diagram_vpc_peering.png

Best,
Aditya Rao
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.inference



From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@icare.health>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.inference>, Aaron Patel <aaron.patel@icare.health>
Cc: procurement@icare.health
Date: Thu, 03 Dec 2026 09:25:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Recap: Security evaluation & hosting options for iCare

Aditya — thanks for the clear summary. A few clarifying items from our security and procurement teams:

1) Preferred hosting: our default preference is Dedicated or Private deployment for PHI workloads. Hosted may be acceptable only if we can validate logical separation, customer-managed KMS, and contractually guaranteed BAA terms.
2) Evidence needed: we need the SOC 2 Type II report (redacted is fine), an index of evidence items mapped to common criteria (logging, change control, vulnerability management), and a list of subprocessors.
3) Logs & PHI: clarify whether request/response logs can be configured to exclude or redact PHI, and the default retention period for request traces. Also need an export path for audit (CSV or SIEM integration).
4) Timeline: target vendor selection by the end of December; procurement wants a signed BAA + basic evidence by next week to start internal approval.

Can your team confirm the following explicitly (short bullet answers would be great):
- Does Hosted support customer-managed KMS?
- What is the shortest supported log retention window and can it be shortened to 30 days?
- For Dedicated, does Redwood support network-level VPC peering and private IPs for model endpoints?

Happy to set a technical call — our infra lead (Aaron) is available Dec 10–11 in the morning PST.

Thanks,
Samantha Lee
Director of Vendor Risk | iCare Health
samantha.lee@icare.health
